title: Matrices & determinants
summary: Matrices are rectangular arrays you can add, scale, transpose, and multiply (with the inner sizes matching). For square matrices the determinant decides everything: $\det A\ne0$ iff $A$ is invertible. This topic covers matrix operations, the transpose law $(AB)^{\top}=B^{\top}A^{\top}$, special matrices (identity, diagonal, nilpotent), computing $2\times2$ and $3\times3$ determinants (Sarrus, Laplace), their properties and Binet's theorem $\det(AB)=\det A\det B$, and inverting a matrix via the cofactor/adjugate (chess-board) method.
objectives: Add, scale, transpose and multiply matrices, respecting size compatibility
Apply the order-reversing transpose law $(AB)^{\top}=B^{\top}A^{\top}$ and recognise non-commutativity of products
Identify identity, diagonal, and nilpotent matrices and compute powers $A^n$
Compute $2\times2$ and $3\times3$ determinants via the $ad-bc$ rule, Sarrus, and Laplace cofactor expansion
Use the determinant properties and Binet's theorem $\det(AB)=\det A\det B$
Decide when a matrix is invertible ($\det A\ne0$) and compute the inverse via the cofactor/adjugate method

blocks.text: A **matrix** is a rectangular array of real numbers. If it has $m$ rows and $n$ columns we say its **size** is $m\times n$, and we write $A\in\mathbb{R}^{m,n}$ (the first superscript is always the number of rows). The number in row $i$, column $j$ is the **entry** $a_{ij}$, so a generic matrix is written $A=(a_{ij})$. When $m=n$ the matrix is **square**. A matrix with one row is a row vector; one with a single column is a column vector — both with $n$ entries live in $\mathbb{R}^n$.

Two matrices can be **added** only if they have the same size; you add **entry by entry**. **Scalar multiplication** $cA$ multiplies every entry by $c\in\mathbb{R}$; in particular $0A$ is the **null matrix** $0$, and $-B=(-1)B$ so subtraction is $A-B=A+(-B)$. Addition is commutative: $A+B=B+A$.

The **transpose** $A^{\top}$ (also written $\,^{t}A$) is obtained by interchanging rows and columns: $(A^{\top})_{ij}=a_{ji}$. If $A\in\mathbb{R}^{m,n}$ then $A^{\top}\in\mathbb{R}^{n,m}$. It is self-inverse, $(A^{\top})^{\top}=A$, and respects sums, $(A+B)^{\top}=A^{\top}+B^{\top}$.

The product $AB$ is defined **only when the number of columns of $A$ equals the number of rows of $B$**. If $A\in\mathbb{R}^{m,n}$ has rows $r_1,\dots,r_m$ and $B\in\mathbb{R}^{n,p}$ has columns $c_1,\dots,c_p$, then $AB\in\mathbb{R}^{m,p}$ has entries $(AB)_{ij}=r_i\cdot c_j$ (the dot product of row $i$ of $A$ with column $j$ of $B$).

Matrix multiplication is **NOT commutative**: in general $AB\ne BA$ (and $BA$ may not even be defined). Worse, $AB$ can be the **zero matrix even when neither $A$ nor $B$ is zero**, e.g. $\begin{pmatrix}1&1\\2&2\end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix}1&-1\\-1&1\end{pmatrix}=\begin{pmatrix}0&0\\0&0\end{pmatrix}$. So never cancel matrix factors as if they were numbers.

A square matrix is **diagonal** if $a_{ij}=0$ whenever $i\ne j$ (only the main diagonal $\searrow$ can be nonzero). The **identity matrix** $I_n$ is the diagonal matrix with all diagonal entries equal to $1$; it acts as a neutral element: $I_mA=A=AI_n$. Powers of a square matrix are $A^2=AA$, $A^3=AAA$, with $A^0=I_n$; powers of the *same* matrix commute, $A^mA^n=A^nA^m$. A matrix with $A^m=0$ for some $m$ is called **nilpotent**.

A square matrix $A\in\mathbb{R}^{n,n}$ is **invertible** (or **nonsingular**) if there exists a matrix $A^{-1}$ with $AA^{-1}=I_n$. When it exists the inverse is **unique** and automatically satisfies $A^{-1}A=I_n$ as well.

For a $2\times2$ matrix there is an explicit recipe. If $A=\begin{pmatrix}a&b\\c&d\end{pmatrix}$ then $A$ is invertible **iff** $ad-bc\ne0$, and in that case the inverse is the formula below. The quantity $ad-bc$ is the **determinant** of $A$.
To every square matrix $A\in\mathbb{R}^{n,n}$ we attach a number $\det A$ (also written $|A|$), a function of its entries. The central theorem:
**$\det A\ne0$ if and only if $A$ is invertible.** This single fact links determinant, inverse, and (later) rank and linear independence. If $\det A=0$ the matrix is **singular** and has no inverse.

For a $3\times3$ matrix there are two equivalent ways to compute $\det A$. **Sarrus' rule**: copy the first two columns to the right and add the three $\searrow$ diagonal products, then subtract the three $\nearrow$ diagonal products. **Laplace expansion along the first row** (cofactor expansion) uses the $2\times2$ minors built from the last two rows:

**Properties of the determinant** (proved from the formula, valid for rows or columns since $\det A=\det A^{\top}$):
- Multiplying one row by $c$ multiplies $\det A$ by $c$.
- $\det(cA)=c^{n}\det A$ for an $n\times n$ matrix (one factor $c$ per row).
- Swapping two rows changes the **sign** of $\det A$.
- If one row is a multiple of another (or two rows equal), then $\det A=0$.
- $\det A=\det A^{\top}$.

**Binet's theorem** (multiplicativity): $\det(AB)=\det A\,\det B$. Hence $\det(A^{-1})=1/\det A$.
The determinant is **NOT additive**: $\det(A+B)\ne\det A+\det B$ in general. Also $\det(cA)=c^{n}\det A$, not $c\det A$ — students often forget the power $n$ equals the size of the matrix.

Let $A_{ij}$ be the matrix obtained by **deleting row $i$ and column $j$** of $A$. The **cofactor matrix** $\widetilde{A}$ has entries $\widetilde{a}_{ij}=(-1)^{i+j}\det(A_{ij})$ — i.e. each entry is replaced by the determinant of its complementary matrix, with signs alternating chess-board style $\begin{pmatrix}+&-&+\\-&+&-\\+&-&+\end{pmatrix}$. The transpose $\widetilde{A}^{\top}$ is the **adjugate** (or adjoint).

steps.label: Check invertibility
steps.text: Compute $\det A$. If $\det A=0$, stop: $A$ is not invertible. Otherwise continue.

steps.label: Cofactors
steps.text: For each position $(i,j)$ delete row $i$ and column $j$, compute the minor $\det(A_{ij})$, and attach the sign $(-1)^{i+j}$. This gives the cofactor matrix $\widetilde{A}$.

blocks.title: Inverse of a 3×3 matrix
blocks.text:
Let $A=\begin{pmatrix}1&2&3\0&1&2\1&4&0\end{pmatrix}$. Cofactor matrix (each minor with chess-board sign):
$$\widetilde{A}=\begin{pmatrix}-8&2&-1\12&-3&-2\1&-2&1\end{pmatrix}.$$
Then $A\,\widetilde{A}^{\top}=-9\,I_3$, so $\det A=-9$ and
$$A^{-1}=\frac{1}{-9}\begin{pmatrix}-8&12&1\2&-3&-2\-1&-2&1\end{pmatrix}=\begin{pmatrix}\tfrac{8}{9}&-\tfrac{4}{3}&-\tfrac{1}{9}\-\tfrac{2}{9}&\tfrac{1}{3}&\tfrac{2}{9}\\tfrac{1}{9}&\tfrac{2}{3}&-\tfrac{1}{9}\end{pmatrix}.$$

Quick checks: a triangular or diagonal matrix has determinant equal to the **product of its diagonal entries**. A matrix with a zero row, a repeated row, or one row a multiple of another always has $\det=0$ (so it is NOT invertible).

question.prompt: For which values of $a\in\mathbb{R}$ is $A=\begin{pmatrix}1&a\\a&1\end{pmatrix}$ invertible?
question.explanation:
$\det A=1-a^2$. Invertible iff $\det A
e0$, i.e. $1-a^2
e0$, so $a
e\pm1$. A is wrong because $a=\pm1$ give $\det=0$; C and D misidentify the condition.

question.prompt: If $A$ is a $3\times3$ matrix with $\det A=4$, what is $\det(2A)$?
question.explanation: $\det(cA)=c^{n}\det A$ with $n=3$, so $\det(2A)=2^3\cdot4=8\cdot4=32$. A treats it as $2\det A$; B and D use the wrong power.
